_meta.citation: Pargament, K. I., Smith, B. W., Koenig, H. G., & Perez, L. (1998). Patterns of positive and negative religious coping with major life stressors. Journal for the Scientific Study of Religion, 37(4), 710–724. DOI: 10.2307/1388152
_meta.shortScaleReference: Pargament, K. I., Feuille, M., & Burdzy, D. (2011). The Brief RCOPE: Current psychometric status of a short measure of religious coping. Religions, 2(1), 51–76. DOI: 10.3390/rel2010051
_meta.responseFormat: 4-point Likert: 1=Not at all, 2=Somewhat, 3=Quite a bit, 4=A great deal
_meta.validity: Concurrent validity established against the full 105-item RCOPE (r = .91 positive; r = .87 negative)

egyptianAdaptation.status: Culturally adapted for Egyptian Muslim context
egyptianAdaptation.adaptationNotes: Items translated and culturally validated for Arabic-speaking Muslims. 'God' references retained as 'الله' (Allah). Items referencing 'church' adapted to 'mosque/masjid'. Fatwa references added where appropriate.
egyptianAdaptation.arabicValidation: Abdel-Khalek & Lester (2012) validated Arabic religious coping measures in Egyptian samples: Journal of Muslim Mental Health, 6(2). DOI: 10.3998/jmmh.10381607.0006.201

_meta.clinicalNote: This instrument is used for educational and research purposes within EAL. It does NOT constitute a clinical assessment. Users scoring high on negative religious coping (≥21) are automatically routed to the HelpSeekingWizard and shown Egyptian mental health resources.
_meta.mindframeGuidelines: Per Mindframe (Australia) and adapted for Egyptian context: always pair religious coping content with professional resource referral. Never use coping scales to pathologize religious practice.

config.name: Brief Religious Coping Scale (Brief-RCOPE)
config.description: The following questions ask about ways you cope with stress, problems, or difficult situations using your faith. There are no right or wrong answers.
config.description_ar: الأسئلة التالية تسأل عن الطرق التي تتعامل بها مع الضغوط والمشكلات والمواقف الصعبة من خلال إيمانك. لا توجد إجابات صحيحة أو خاطئة.
config.instruction: Think about a difficult situation you have faced recently. Rate how much you used each of the following to cope with it.
config.instruction_ar: فكّر في موقف صعب واجهته مؤخراً. قيّم مدى استخدامك لكل مما يلي للتعامل معه.

items.text_en: Looked for a stronger connection with God (Allah).
items.egyptianContext: Common Egyptian expression of faith during hardship — seeking closeness to Allah (تقرب إلى الله)
Aligned with the Islamic concept of Rahmah (رحمة) — God's mercy as a source of comfort

items.text_en: Sought help from God (Allah) in letting go of my anger.
items.text_ar: طلبت من الله المساعدة للتخلص من غضبي.
items.egyptianContext: Islamic teaching on controlling anger (كظم الغيظ) as a virtue — Hadith: 'Do not get angry' (Bukhari)

items.text_en: Tried to put my plans into action together with God (Allah).
items.text_ar: حاولت تنفيذ خططي بالتوكل على الله.
items.egyptianContext: Tawakkul (التوكل) — reliance on God while taking action. Islamic principle of effort + trust in God.

items.text_en: Tried to see how God (Allah) might be trying to strengthen me in this situation.
items.text_ar: حاولت أن أرى كيف يمكن أن يكون الله يقوّيني من خلال هذا الموقف.
items.egyptianContext: Concept of Ibtila' (ابتلاء) — trials as divine tests that build character. Common Egyptian religious frame.

items.text_en: Asked for forgiveness of my sins.
items.egyptianContext: Istighfar (الاستغفار) — seeking divine forgiveness as a coping strategy. Highly prevalent in Egyptian culture.

items.text_en: Focused on religion to stop worrying about my problems.
items.text_ar: تركزت على الدين لأوقف قلقي حول مشكلاتي.
items.egyptianContext: Dhikr (ذكر الله) and prayer as anxiety management — common coping in Egyptian Muslim households.

items.text_en: Wondered whether God (Allah) had abandoned me.
items.text_ar: تساءلت إذا كان الله قد تخلى عني.
items.egyptianContext: Feeling abandoned by God can signal spiritual crisis. In Islamic theology, this is associated with Waswas (وسواس — whispers of Shaytan). High score triggers gentle psychoeducation.

items.text_en: Felt punished by God (Allah) for my lack of devotion.
items.text_ar: شعرت بأن الله يعاقبني بسبب ضعف إيماني.
items.egyptianContext: Common in contexts of illness or loss. Can lead to guilt-based avoidance of religious community. Important to address with Islamic scholars' perspective (Dar Al-Ifta resources).

items.text_en: Wondered what I did for God (Allah) to punish me like this.
items.text_ar: تساءلت ماذا فعلت لكي يعاقبني الله هكذا.
items.egyptianContext: Self-blame framing in religious context. Can reinforce depression. EAL links this to myth-autopsy.tsx for the 'depression is punishment' myth.
Theological doubt during crisis. EAL routes high scorers to fatwa-context page for Dar Al-Ifta's perspective on God's love (رحمة الله الواسعة).

items.text_en: Wondered whether my mosque/religious community had abandoned me.
items.text_ar: تساءلت إذا كان مجتمعي الديني قد تخلى عني.
items.egyptianContext: Adapted from original 'church' to 'mosque/masjid'. Social isolation from religious community is a significant risk factor for Egyptian Muslims.

items.text_en: Decided the devil was making this happen.
items.text_ar: قررت أن الشيطان هو من يتسبب في هذا.
items.egyptianContext: Externalizing blame to Shaytan (الشيطان). While Islamic theology does acknowledge Shaytan's role, excessive attribution can prevent taking personal responsibility or seeking treatment.

items.text_en: Questioned the power of God (Allah) to help me.
items.text_ar: شككت في قدرة الله على مساعدتي.
items.egyptianContext: Doubting God's omnipotence (القدرة الإلهية) during severe stress. High score on this item warrants immediate HelpSeekingWizard activation.

interpretation.7–14: Low positive religious coping — user may not be drawing on faith as a resource
interpretation.22–28: High positive religious coping — faith is a central coping resource

interpretation.7–14: Low negative religious coping — no significant spiritual struggle
interpretation.15–20: Moderate negative religious coping — some spiritual struggle present
interpretation.21–28: High negative religious coping — significant spiritual struggle — CLINICAL ALERT: Route to HelpSeekingWizard + crisis resources

clinicalAlert.action: Trigger HelpSeekingWizard with Egyptian mental health resources

trajectory.description: EAL tracks Brief-RCOPE trajectory across 4 time points

high_positive_low_negative.label: Secure Believer
high_positive_low_negative.description: Faith is a genuine resource. EAL reinforces this through Phase 3 Islamic Literacy content.

high_positive_high_negative.label: Struggling Believer
high_positive_high_negative.description: Faith is important but causing conflict. Route to fatwa-context and hadith-check for theological clarity.

low_positive_low_negative.label: Non-religious Coper
low_positive_low_negative.description: Religion is not a primary coping resource. EAL respects this — route to secular mental health tools.

low_positive_high_negative.label: Spiritual Crisis
low_positive_high_negative.description: CRITICAL — faith is causing harm without providing comfort. Immediate HelpSeekingWizard activation.
